mūla.devanāgarī: अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः | कथम् एतद् विजानीयां त्वम् आदौ प्रोक्तवान् इति
mūla.iast: aparaṃ bhavato janma paraṃ janma vivasvataḥ | katham etad vijānīyāṃ tvam ādau proktavān iti
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 4 (Jñāna-Karma-Sannyāsa-Yoga (The Yoga of Renunciation of Action in Knowledge)), verse 4
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: । परं पूर्वं सर्गादौ जन्म उत्पत्तिः विवस्वतः आदित्यस्य। तत् कथम् एतत् विजानीयाम् अविरुद्धार्थतया यः त्व मेव आदौ प्रोक्त
परं प्राक्कालीनं विवस्वतो जन्म तस्मात्तवाधुनिकत्वाच्चिरंतनाय विवस्वते त्वमादौ योगं प्रोक्तवानित्येतत्कथमहं विजानीयां ज्
। परं पूर्वं सर्गादौ जन्म उत्पत्तिः विवस्वतः आदित्यस्य। तत् कथम् एतत् विजानीयाम् अविरुद्धार्थतया यः त्व मेव आदौ प्रोक्त
परं प्राक्कालीनं विवस्वतो जन्म तस्मात्तवाधुनिकत्वाच्चिरंतनाय विवस्वते त्वमादौ योगं प्रोक्तवानित्येतत्कथमहं विजानीयां ज्
एव आदौ प्रोक्तवान् इति कथम् एतद् असम्भावनीयं विशेषण यथार्थं जानीयाम्
इति कथम् एतद् असम्भावनीयं विशेषण यथार्थं जानीयाम्

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: Why does Arjuna ask about this now — after three chapters of ethical and metaphysical instruction — rather than at the outset?
Is Arjuna genuinely confused, or is this a Socratic move designed to elicit a self-disclosure from Kṛṣṇa?
What does it mean for a teaching (yoga) to be transmitted across cosmic time — does the content of the yoga change, or only its vehicle?
The verse uses 'apara' and 'para' (recent and prior) — does this temporal pair carry any connection to the Upaniṣadic apara/para vidyā distinction (lower/higher knowledge)?
If Kṛṣṇa's answer will be 'I have many births but remember them all' (4.5), what does that claim require us to accept about the relationship between memory, identity, and the body?
How do the six schools differ in whether they read Arjuna as confused (advaita, Śrīdhara) versus strategically asking (Rāmānuja, Vallabha)?
What is at stake philosophically in the distinction between 'teaching in a past body' versus 'teaching as the unchanging ātman clothed in successive bodies'?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself saying 'I am not the same person I was ten years ago' and using that change as an excuse not to honour a commitment, notice the confusion: the body and personality shift, but the witness-awareness (sākṣin) that knows all those changes is continuous. The Advaita reading asks: who is the 'I' across the time-gap — the body or the aware presence behind it?
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Before raising a hard question with someone you love or respect, examine whether your question is actually an invitation for them to reveal themselves more fully. Rāmānuja's Arjuna already knows the answer; he asks because the asking itself is an act of relational kainkarya (devoted service) that allows the beloved to speak. Use questions as offerings, not just information requests.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's compressed reading is a reminder that the purpose of asking about great teachers' origins is not intellectual curiosity but recognition of their primordial authority. When you study any tradition, orient your first questions toward 'what is the source of this person's authority?' rather than 'does this make logical sense to me?' — the māhātmya question precedes the epistemological one.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's Arjuna uses an 'impossible' situation as a pretext to invite the beloved to speak. In relationships, sometimes the most generative thing you can offer is a question you already half-know the answer to — a question whose real function is to create the space for the other to be fully, joyfully themselves. The 'pretext question' is an act of grace.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's Arjuna confesses epistemic limitation plainly: 'how could I be capable of knowing?' (jñātum śaknuyām). This is the appropriate posture before a teacher whose knowledge exceeds our category of time. Cultivate the habit of stating your confusion as a specific incapacity rather than a general scepticism — not 'I don't believe you' but 'I cannot yet see how this fits together; please show me.'
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana identifies two compounding errors: thinking the teacher is bound by time (anityatva) and therefore forgetful (asarvajñatva). In modern terms: we discount advice from someone older or younger than ourselves on the grounds that 'they can't know what my world is like.' But wisdom that has survived multiple contexts is precisely the wisdom least bound by any one context. Test your dismissals: are they tracking a real limitation in the teacher, or projecting a limitation you assume time imposes?
